title: Turkey Shoot

jokes.text: Don't judge me for what I'm about to ask you to do.
jokes.setup: David opens with an ominous, dramatic plea — the audience braces for something serious or scandalous.
jokes.punchline: The 'terrible' thing is just a bug trapped under a glass that he needs moral support to remove.
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection opener that sets up the David-as-high-strung-dramatic-person characterization early. The gap between the gravitas of the ask and the mundane reality is the joke. Well-structured but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So I looked up what it could be, and apparently there's something called a 'demonic cricket.'

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: A demonic cricket. And according to this forum, they come in chunks.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: They come in chunks, David.

jokes.text: I Googled that bug. It's some sort of demonic cricket that 'takes chunks out of your skin when provoked.' Chunks is the word that Google used.
jokes.setup: Stevie observes a bug under a glass and treats it as unremarkable.
jokes.punchline: David has already done full research, emphasizing the word 'chunks' as if Google's diction is the terrifying part.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'chunks is the word that Google used' is the gem here — it's so perfectly David to fixate on the exact word choice rather than the danger itself. Strong character integration and highly quotable. The deadpan delivery of citing Google as an authority on demonic crickets is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I have a phobia of bugs. Like, a real phobia. I can't... I can't be around them. Just the thought of them crawling into my mouth while I'm sleeping, nesting in my teeth, laying eggs in my gums... it's...

jokes.text: I have a thing about bugs with milky exoskeletons. I have this irrational fear they are gonna crawl into my mouth and nest at night.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks what David wants her to do about the bug.
jokes.punchline: David's explanation escalates into a hyper-specific phobia about bugs nesting in his mouth at night, delivered with the casual acknowledgment that it's irrational.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'milky exoskeletons' and 'nest at night' is wonderfully grotesque and very David. The self-awareness ('irrational') makes it smarter than a simple gross-out gag. Slightly bloated in delivery but works as character building.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, maybe a bug will nest in your mouth while you sleep.

jokes.text: I think I just need moral support while I slip a piece of paper under the glass with the bug and take the trapped bug and walk it outside.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if he wants her to kill it — the obvious, practical solution.
jokes.punchline: David has an elaborate, humane protocol that requires moral support — for a bug removal.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the gap between the complexity of David's protocol and the simplicity of the task. Slightly over-explained which hurts the economy score, but character-specific and charming.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you're not wrong.

jokes.text: Oh! Oh my God! It's fleeing! — Whoa, it's fast! — It's fleeing into the bathroom. That's why you use the cup method, because you'd keep it contained! Now it's just all out!
jokes.setup: David has been carefully constructing his plan to humanely remove the bug.
jokes.punchline: The bug escapes under its own power, making all of David's careful planning irrelevant; David's panic escalates rapidly while Stevie observes with bemused detachment.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation beat — the comedy of David's meticulous plan being immediately defeated by the bug's agency. 'It's fleeing!' is a funny word choice suggesting the bug has intent. The physical chaos implied by the dialogue lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello?! Where is my towel? David! David, where is my towel?!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's in the dryer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The dryer? David, I am standing here, soaking wet, like some sort of... drowned arctic fox, and my towel is in the dryer?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is unacceptable. Do you know what this feels like? This is what I imagine homelessness feels like.

jokes.text: Well... I guess I don't even need to be here.
jokes.setup: The bug escapes and David panics — Stevie was called in specifically to help with this.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, unbothered acknowledgment that her presence has become entirely pointless.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — five words that capture Stevie's dry wit and her role as the deadpan observer to David's chaos. Strong character integration; this is exactly how Stevie operates. The understatement is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So when someone says they're 'pitching a tent,' it's just a cute way of saying—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Getting a boner.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I was going to say it more delicately, but sure.

jokes.text: Now it's hiding somewhere in the darkness. Waiting for nightfall.
jokes.setup: The bug has fled into the bathroom and David is processing this.
jokes.punchline: David anthropomorphizes the bug as a strategic predator with a temporal plan — it's 'waiting for nightfall' to crawl into his mouth.
jokes.explanation: A quietly excellent line. David's genuine terror has twisted the bug into a patient, villainous creature. 'Waiting for nightfall' is a horror-movie phrase applied to a cricket, and it directly callbacks his fear of nighttime nesting. The delivery implied here is two short, dramatic sentences which gives it great structure and rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: These towels are absolutely filthy. I cannot use these.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, those are the clean towels.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they smell like a bug died in them.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A bug did die in them.

jokes.text: Not with your mouth open anyway.
jokes.setup: David says 'I can't sleep here' in genuine terror.
jokes.punchline: Stevie calmly notes that he CAN sleep there — just with his mouth closed — which simultaneously validates and mocks his bug-nesting fear.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the cold open and one of the best in the episode. Perfect economy (six words), perfect structure — it works as a callback to David's specific mouth-nesting fear, as a deadpan understatement, and as a character beat for Stevie. She doesn't dismiss his fear; she technically validates it while completely deflating its drama. This approaches the 8.0 anchor in construction quality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A bug? A bug?! Do you understand what you've done? You've taken a Frette towel—a Frette towel—and used it as an insecticide! This isn't a towel anymore, it's a crime scene! I can't even look at it without feeling like I need to be decontaminated. It's been violated. We've all been violated.

jokes.text: You know, sometimes I forget what life was like before I knew you. Okay, bye-bye! Bye. Yup. 'Kay. Hope you catch it!
jokes.setup: David has been utterly dramatic about a single cricket. Stevie says she sometimes forgets what life was like before she knew David.
jokes.punchline: The 'compliment' is dripping with irony — life before David was presumably quieter and bug-drama-free. Then her breezy exit and 'Hope you catch it!' twists the knife.
jokes.explanation: A warm, ironic send-off from Stevie. The line works on two levels — genuine affection and total exasperation. The cheerful escalation of 'bye-bye! Bye. Yup. 'Kay.' is a nice comedic rhythm. 'Hope you catch it!' lands as the parting shot.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A spa day? I don't have time for such pedestrian pursuits. I'm far too busy maintaining this level of fabulousness.

jokes.text: I asked this kid to come to the front of the class and solve this math equation and he was 'pitching a tent.' He had a boner!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is apparently in the middle of a story with Johnny and Moira at what seems to be a casual morning meeting.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn, who is a schoolteacher and the wife of the mayor, enthusiastically and shamelessly explains that a student had an erection in her class — to the Roses, who are visibly caught off guard.
jokes.explanation: Works primarily as cringe comedy and character establishment for Jocelyn — she's warm, oblivious to propriety, and tells wildly inappropriate stories. The 'He had a boner!' clarification (in case anyone missed the euphemism) is a nice comedic beat showing her complete lack of filter.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, the weight she's carrying...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, she does seem to be retaining water. I have a wonderful colonics specialist in the city.

jokes.text: And it can't be easy having a teacher like myself, so... What was your question again?
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is sympathizing with young boys who get erections in her class because she's so attractive.
jokes.punchline: She trails off into self-congratulation and then completely forgets what the conversation was about.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's breezy vanity ('a teacher like myself') is funny, and the pivot to forgetting the original question suggests the whole story was really just an excuse to talk about how attractive she is. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Find me a towel immediately or I will air dry naked in the town square.

jokes.text: There's a towel on the back of the door. — No, that one has dirt on it! — That's not dirt. I used it to kill a bug.
jokes.setup: Moira is outraged that her towel has 'dirt' on it.
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals he used it to kill a bug — a callback to David's bug crisis from the cold open, meaning the bug DID get killed (by the least noble method), and Moira now knows.
jokes.explanation: Elegant callback that resolves the cold open bug plot in the most mundane and anticlimactic way possible. Johnny killed it with a towel — the exact opposite of David's elaborate humane removal plan. Moira's scream upon learning this is the perfect button. Good economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What's in the Meadow Harvest smoothie?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, it's made with meadow harvest ingredients.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but what ingredients?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: The ones we harvest from the meadow.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Twyla, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: That's why it's called Meadow Harvest.

jokes.text: Moira screams in anguish upon learning the towel 'dirt' is bug residue.
jokes.setup: Moira was already upset about the towel having 'dirt' on it.
jokes.punchline: Learning it's bug remains triggers a full theatrical scream — her reaction is wildly disproportionate to the news.
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical scream is a character beat — she responds to minor indignities with catastrophic emotion. Works because it's so perfectly Moira. High character integration but not particularly original as a comedic device.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Is it fruity?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, it's made of fruit.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but is it fruity?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: It's as fruity as a smoothie made of fruit would be.

jokes.text: Let's not and say we did.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn cheerfully offers Moira a salon recommendation for pampering and unwinding.
jokes.punchline: Moira's crisp, withering dismissal using the old-fashioned idiom, delivered with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: A classic Moira line — maximum dismissal in minimum words, delivered with aristocratic confidence. The idiom itself is slightly dated which makes it feel very Moira. Strong economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I would avoid that smoothie if I were you. Let's just say Twyla's digestive system is like a theme park, and you do not want to be on that ride.

jokes.text: Could alleviate some of that weight you're carrying around. — So now I'm fat and stressed?
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests pampering could help with 'the weight' Moira is carrying — meaning emotional/psychological stress.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately interprets it as a comment on her physical weight, combining two insults in one misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: A familiar comedy of miscommunication but executed efficiently. Moira's 'So now I'm fat AND stressed?' neatly packages the double misinterpretation. Not the most original premise but clean structure.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I've been recommending smoothies to patients for years. They're great for digestion, energy, mental clarity...

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, I prescribe them. Medically.

jokes.text: Or I will strip out of this robe and I will air dry in the street!
jokes.setup: Moira is upset and demands a towel immediately.
jokes.punchline: Her threat escalates to public nudity — an absurdly disproportionate response to not having a clean towel.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira escalation — her threats are always theatrical and disproportionate. The specificity of 'air dry in the street' is funnier than a generic threat. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're like, a dog doctor?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm a veterinarian, yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but I mean, it's basically the same thing as being a regular doctor, right? Like, you went to medical school and everything?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Veterinary school, actually.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but like, how different could it be? I mean, a body is a body.

jokes.text: The Meadow Harvest smoothie exchange — Twyla explains it 'changes every day, depends on her mood, all organic, earthy,' then when pressed on fruity vs. vegetably, admits 'No, I don't know.'
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to understand what she's ordering — a reasonable customer question.
jokes.punchline: Twyla has essentially no concrete information about her own menu item. 'Meadow Harvest' means nothing specific. She doesn't know what's in it today. The name is the description.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's cheerful incompetence is a running character gag. The extended non-answer to a simple question, punctuated by 'No, I don't know,' captures her pleasant obliviousness perfectly. Slightly runs long but Twyla's character is well-served.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, stop. I mean, I know, but like, stop.

jokes.text: Meadow Harvest is exactly how it sounds. — It just changes every day.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if 'Meadow Harvest' refers to anything in particular.
jokes.punchline: Twyla confirms it's exactly how it sounds... which means nothing, because 'Meadow Harvest' is pure nonsense as a menu description.
jokes.explanation: The confirmation that the name is self-explanatory — when it explains nothing — is the purest form of Twyla's logic. 'Exactly how it sounds' when it sounds like nothing is a sharp little absurdist beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, it's actually not that bad.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Wait, give it a few minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, I think you were just being dramatic, Ted. This is actually really—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh God. Oh God, no. No, no, no.

jokes.text: What you just ordered is gonna run through you like Niagara Falls.
jokes.setup: Ted introduces himself to Alexis by warning her about the smoothie she just ordered.
jokes.punchline: His opening gambit with a beautiful woman is to discuss the laxative effect of her drink.
jokes.explanation: A funny meet-cute where the opening line is about diarrhea. 'Niagara Falls' is a vivid, funny simile. Works as character introduction — Ted is confident, straightforward, a little oblivious to social convention despite good intentions.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My idea of nature is a luxury music festival where the bathrooms are immaculate and there's a tent dedicated solely to artisanal mineral water.

jokes.text: I'm... I'm... I'm kidding. I'm actually not. I've absolutely done that... With her smoothies.
jokes.setup: Ted has just warned Alexis about the smoothie's laxative effect.
jokes.punchline: He tries to walk it back ('kidding') then immediately un-walks it back, confirming he has personally experienced diarrhea from Twyla's smoothies.
jokes.explanation: The false start and immediate reversal ('I'm kidding. I'm actually not.') is a great comedic rhythm. The confession that he's prescribed the smoothies to constipated patients elevates this beyond a simple gross-out joke — it establishes his professional competence while maintaining the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know what you mean by that. I enjoy plenty of activities. I have hobbies. I'm very outdoorsy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I went to a SoulCycle class once. I own a Patagonia vest. I have spent a considerable amount of time in nature.

jokes.text: So are you a doctor or... ? — I'm a veterinarian. — Oh! It's basically the same thing.
jokes.setup: Ted has been offering medical-adjacent advice. Alexis asks if he's a doctor.
jokes.punchline: He's a vet. Alexis immediately validates this with 'It's basically the same thing' — a wonderfully oblivious equation of human and animal medicine.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's 'It's basically the same thing' is a perfect character beat — she's not being mean, she genuinely doesn't understand the difference, or doesn't care to. The complete confidence with which she says it is what makes it land. High character integration and economy. Very Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not concerned about the hunting part. I'm concerned about what one wears to hunt. Do you understand how orange clashes with my complexion?

jokes.text: (Feigning humility) 'What? No. I'm sure there's like another girl... somewhere.'
jokes.setup: Ted says Alexis is the most beautiful girl this town has ever seen.
jokes.punchline: Alexis performs false modesty so thin it's transparent — 'I'm sure there's like another girl... somewhere' is phrased with maximum uncertainty, suggesting she really doesn't think there is.
jokes.explanation: The ellipsis and 'somewhere' do all the work — Alexis's self-awareness about her own beauty, delivered as faux-humility, is quintessentially Alexis. Strong character integration; could only work for this character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This duvet cover is a labyrinth of despair! These corners are playing games with me, and I am LOSING.

jokes.text: I feel good about this one.
jokes.setup: Ted has asked Alexis on a date. He gets the smoothie she's trying — it's bad.
jokes.punchline: His self-satisfied 'I feel good about this one' plays as comedic confidence both about the date and perhaps ironically about the terrible smoothie.
jokes.explanation: Short, functional beat. Works as confident character moment for Ted. The ambiguity about whether it refers to the date or the smoothie (which we then see is terrible) adds a small layer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I don't understand. A mean scalp massage? Why would anyone want their scalp to be mean to them? I go to the salon to be treated with kindness and respect, not to have my scalp develop a personality disorder.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's a little less fruity but a little more... something. Mm! Mm... (Giggles and spits)
jokes.setup: The entire Meadow Harvest smoothie sequence has been building — Twyla can't describe it, Ted has warned it's laxative.
jokes.punchline: Alexis drinks it, and it's so bad she spits it out — the callback payoff to both the mystery smoothie and Ted's warning.
jokes.explanation: The physical payoff to the smoothie setup. The giggles-then-spit is a nice comedic beat. Works as a visual gag resolution. Not groundbreaking but satisfying as a callback conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm going out tonight. There's this guy, and we're going to grab drinks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's nice.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, so... what are you doing tonight?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to be here. Alone. Which I prefer.

jokes.text: No, I don't have a thing about bugs. It was one bug, and that bug was milky.
jokes.setup: Stevie refers to David's 'thing about bugs' from the cold open.
jokes.punchline: David's denial followed by the qualifier 'that bug was milky' — as if milkiness is a legitimate and universally recognized exception that justifies his behavior.
jokes.explanation: 'That bug was milky' is a quotable little gem. The logic is: I don't have a general bug phobia, I have a very specific and reasonable milky-bug phobia, and since this bug was milky, my reaction was proportionate. The self-assured delivery of an absurd distinction is perfectly David. High quotability and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Competence.

jokes.text: I do Coachella every year, so...
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if David is 'a man at one with nature' after his bug panic, noting he apparently goes to Roland's turkey shoot.
jokes.punchline: David cites Coachella — a music festival in the desert — as his nature credential. This is simultaneously true and absurd as evidence of being outdoorsy.
jokes.explanation: Coachella as a nature credential is a perfectly calibrated piece of character comedy. It tells us everything about David's previous life — he considers a luxury festival in the desert to be 'outdoors experience.' The trailing 'so...' adds confidence. Very high character integration; this joke could only belong to David Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Did you know that Meryl Streep can speak like fifteen languages? I read that somewhere. Or maybe it was just that she's really good at accents. Anyway, she's amazing.

jokes.text: I was joking. I have a strong feeling that would not be your thing. — And what do you think 'my thing' is exactly? — High thread count sheets? Colognes that smell like fireplace...
jokes.setup: David has agreed to go hunting. Stevie reveals she was joking.
jokes.punchline: When David demands to know what she thinks his 'thing' is, Stevie offers 'high thread count sheets' and 'colognes that smell like fireplace' — both devastatingly accurate and funny characterizations.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's characterization of David through luxury consumer goods is sharp comedy writing. 'Colognes that smell like fireplace' is a beautifully specific detail — it's a real product category (woody/smoky fragrances) that's become associated with a certain kind of upscale male aesthetic. It's earned because it's observationally true AND character-specific. One of the better jokes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You can't just go traveling with strangers. Anything could happen.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, relax. I'm not traveling with strangers. I'm going on an international blind date.

jokes.text: Nothing would make me more excited than to... Go hunting for... — Turkeys. — Turkeys... with you. So... is this a tweed or a camo situation?
jokes.setup: David has committed to proving he's outdoorsy by going hunting.
jokes.punchline: His manufactured enthusiasm collapses immediately into practical aesthetic concerns — specifically whether he should wear tweed or camouflage.
jokes.explanation: 'Is this a tweed or a camo situation?' is the episode's most quotable line. It perfectly encapsulates David's character — he can only process the hunting trip as a fashion question. The trailing-off enthusiasm ('Nothing would make me more excited than to... Go hunting for...') before Stevie has to supply 'Turkeys' is a nice structural beat. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because Alexis, that's what people do. They talk to each other's parents. It's a normal part of being in a relationship. I know that might be foreign to you, given your track record, but some of us actually maintain connections with our partner's families.

jokes.text: Did anyone actually see this pillow come out of this container? — Seriously?! Go. To. Hell!
jokes.setup: The Roses are struggling with basic household tasks in their motel room.
jokes.punchline: The fury of 'Go. To. Hell!' — directed at a pillow — is Moira treating an inanimate object with the same theatrical contempt she reserves for people.
jokes.explanation: Moira cursing at a pillow is good fish-out-of-water character comedy. People who've never made their own beds encountering vacuum-sealed pillows is a relatable frustration elevated by her extreme reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's wonderful that they're being so civil about it. When your father and I separated, it was absolute carnage.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, there were restraining orders involved.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was a very public dissolution.

jokes.text: I was just booking myself an appointment with Janine at the salon and I said to myself, 'wow, Moira really looks like she needs a pick-me-up,' so I booked you a Mani too! Little girls spa day on me.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn arrives cheerfully at the motel room.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn has made a unilateral decision about Moira's appearance ('really looks like she needs a pick-me-up') while presenting it as a gift. The casualness of 'Little girls spa day on me' to Moira Rose is wonderfully oblivious.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's oblivious generosity — booking Moira an appointment based on how bad she looks, framed as a treat — is good cringe comedy. 'Little girls spa day' applied to Moira is a funny tonal clash.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, your mother and I have always believed that a healthy sexual relationship is the foundation of any marriage. When I was your age, I made a list of all the things I wanted to accomplish in the bedroom, and let me tell you, some of them required equipment we had to special order from a catalog.

jokes.text: May I see a spa treatment menu? — Oh, well, it's just a salon, so they don't really do all that kind of stuff. But she does give a mean scalp massage. — Really mean?
jokes.setup: Moira asks for a spa menu, attempting to engage on familiar luxury terms.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn clarifies it's just a salon. Moira seizes on 'mean scalp massage' with the literal interpretation 'Really mean?' — parsing 'mean' as 'unkind' rather than 'impressive,' revealing the complete culture gap.
jokes.explanation: Moira's literal interpretation of 'mean' (as in aggressive/unpleasant rather than excellent) is a nice bit of wordplay that also reveals her wariness about small-town services. Good economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A dinner date? That's wonderful. Where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I'm sure the ambiance will more than make up for the food.

jokes.text: A polite person would now ask me what I'm doing tonight. — That's what this is about? Sorry. Um... what're you doing tonight?
jokes.setup: Alexis has shared what she's doing tonight (date with Ted). David responds with indifference.
jokes.punchline: Alexis explicitly instructs David on social reciprocity — as if social scripts need to be spelled out for him — and he immediately and sheepishly complies.
jokes.explanation: Captures the sibling dynamic well — Alexis demanding normal social engagement from an oblivious David. Her explicit instruction ('A polite person would now ask...') is funnier than simply saying 'Ask me what I'm doing.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you look like you're going to invade a small country.

jokes.text: I think you'd be good for each other. — Do you? — I think he has everything you're looking for in a husband. — A husband? — What am I looking for in a husband? — Money.
jokes.setup: David suggests Ted and Alexis would be good for each other, building to 'he has everything you're looking for in a husband.'
jokes.punchline: After a beat where we expect something romantic or character-based, David delivers the single-word answer: 'Money.' — and then Alexis's 'Hmm' suggests she's considering this seriously.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is perfect: long setup ('I think you'd be good for each other' → 'A husband?' → 'What am I looking for in a husband?') followed by a one-word punchline. The word 'Money' functions as both a satirical dig at the Roses' pre-fall values AND an ironic commentary given Ted notably lacks a trust fund. The pause before 'Hmm' — Alexis considering this as valid — is the button. Approaches the 8.0 anchor in economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: WHAT?! You've never killed before?! Well, that's nothing to be ashamed of, buddy. I didn't kill my first thing until I was in my thirties!

jokes.text: Did you know Richard Gere's middle name is Tiffany?
jokes.setup: An apparent non sequitur interjected mid-scene.
jokes.punchline: This comes completely out of nowhere. Whether it's true or false (it's false — his middle name is not Tiffany), it's delivered as if it's casual conversation and David is simply filling silence.
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully random David non sequitur that works because of its complete commitment and lack of context. Whether it's a deliberate lie or just something David 'knows,' it's strange and funny. The specificity of 'Tiffany' as a supposedly masculine celebrity's middle name adds to the absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not wearing that. It looks like something a woodsman would die in.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Although... the color is rather neutral. And the utilitarian pockets could work if styled correctly with the right accessories.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know what? I could make this work. It's almost... deconstructed workwear.

jokes.text: It's this little buffet restaurant in Elmdale, but he said that we could order off the menu.
jokes.setup: Alexis describes where Ted is taking her — Antonio's.
jokes.punchline: A buffet restaurant where 'we could order off the menu' is presented as a special privilege — Alexis is recalibrating what counts as a good date, which is funny given her former Bali-with-Leo lifestyle.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in what counts as impressive to someone who used to go to Bali on blind dates — being allowed to order off-menu at a buffet. Alexis's slight pride in this detail is the funny part.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He played Beyoncé music like it was a personal introduction.

jokes.text: I went on a blind date to Bali with Leo, so... I'm pretty sure I'm gonna be fine.
jokes.setup: David expresses concern about Alexis traveling far with someone she just met.
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually drops that she once went to Bali on a blind date with 'Leo' — presumably Leonardo DiCaprio — as evidence of her risk tolerance, which also reveals the absurd scale of her previous life.
jokes.explanation: The casual 'with Leo' — implying DiCaprio without stating it — is excellent. Alexis doesn't explain; she assumes David knows who she means. It's a perfectly calibrated celebrity name-drop that reveals character without feeling like cheap name-dropping because the humor is in Alexis's nonchalance. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: A kiss on the cheek is like a handshake.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Ted shook my hand too.

jokes.text: He doesn't have a trust fund, so, like, that's a refreshing change of pace for me.
jokes.setup: David asks who Ted is.
jokes.punchline: Alexis characterizes Ted's appeal partly by his LACK of a trust fund — framed as a novelty ('refreshing change') — revealing that all her previous romantic partners were wealthy.
jokes.explanation: The irony of 'no trust fund' being listed as an attractive quality — because it's 'refreshing' — works well. It shows Alexis beginning to change while still rooted in her old worldview. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So, uh, how's your sex life?

jokes.text: Speaking of people without trust funds, what about that beardy thing that you spend time with? — What? Mutt? — Yeah. — No. Ew. It's not a thing.
jokes.setup: The conversation about Ted and trust funds segues into David asking about Mutt.
jokes.punchline: David's description of Mutt as 'that beardy thing' (not even a person) is casually dehumanizing and funny. Alexis's 'Ew' denial is equally funny in its thoroughness.
jokes.explanation: 'That beardy thing' is a funny way to reference someone — it reduces Mutt to a single physical characteristic while suggesting David considers him barely worth naming. Alexis's 'Ew' over-denial suggests there IS something there.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: David? He's indoorsy. The guy's a real homebody. Won't go outside, won't do anything. He's pale, he's weak, he's scrimpy.

jokes.text: Because he knows how much I love it when mom and dad interrogate your boyfriends.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks why Ted is in the wrong room.
jokes.punchline: David's dry explanation — delivered with maximum sarcasm — that he loves watching their parents interrogate Alexis's dates.
jokes.explanation: David's sibling schadenfreude expressed through sarcasm. Works as character comedy and sets up the interrogation scene.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know anything about babies. I've never held a baby. I don't want to hold a baby.

jokes.text: Tell me, Ted, are your parents still together? — Uh, no. They divorced when I was in high school. — Oh... alcoholism. — No, I think they just realized that they were better off as friends. — Tsk, tsk. Affair. Poor thing.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira interrogate Ted through their adjoining door.
jokes.punchline: Moira and Johnny immediately assign scandalous explanations (alcoholism, affair) to Ted's divorce story despite his clearly innocent explanation. The 'Poor thing' button is especially good.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best sequences. The Roses' pattern of leaping to the most dramatic explanation ('alcoholism,' 'affair') regardless of evidence is perfectly in character. The escalation — one tragic conclusion, then another — and Moira's pitying 'Poor thing' despite being completely wrong is beautifully constructed. High character integration; this behavior is specifically Rose family. Very quotable and has rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Who does Moira look like?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Tonya Harding.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why would you say that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, there's just something about the eyes.

jokes.text: I had the pleasure of treating your son's panic attack.
jokes.setup: Ted recognizes Johnny when Alexis brings him to the right room.
jokes.punchline: He identifies knowing Johnny through treating David's panic attack — a callback to an earlier episode, also funny as it reduces David to a medical incident.
jokes.explanation: Works as a callback and mild character comedy — David's history of anxiety is gently referenced as his most memorable quality to the vet. Not a big laugh but a nice connective tissue moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, it's not a wig. It's a dead animal that I've trained to sit on my head.

jokes.text: So Antonio's buffet, huh? So many choices, one little plate.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions the restaurant — Antonio's buffet — to the Roses.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's wry commentary on buffet dining ('so many choices, one little plate') as a subtle dig at the date venue, delivered with enough charm to be ambiguous.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's philosophical observation about buffets serves as a veiled (but polite) commentary on the date's venue. 'One little plate' has a nice ring to it. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This isn't a wig, Jocelyn. It's a protective style.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are you saying my hair looks like a wig?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no, I would never—

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Because I don't wear wigs, Moira. I have naturally voluminous hair.

jokes.text: Okay, you know what? Bye, guys. We'll see you in the morning!
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to extract herself and Ted from parental scrutiny.
jokes.punchline: 'We'll see you in the morning' is either Alexis being deliberately provocative (implying she'll stay out all night) or carelessly thoughtless — either way it horrifies her parents.
jokes.explanation: The throwaway 'In the morning!' is funny because of how it lands on everyone else. Johnny's horrified reaction sets up the next beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm wearing this wig because Schitt's Creek is a prison, and I refuse to let it take my hair hostage.

jokes.text: 'In the morning!' Very funny, Alexis! Don't even think about it. Ain't gonna happen. — Ted, not on the first date. — Yes, sir.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just said 'See you in the morning!' provocatively.
jokes.punchline: Both parents immediately and loudly react — Johnny to Ted directly ('not on the first date'), and Ted's meek 'Yes, sir' submission to Johnny is the button.
jokes.explanation: Ted's 'Yes, sir' is the punchline — he's so agreeable that he doesn't even push back on the absurdity of taking dating orders from Alexis's dad on a first date. Good character moment for Ted.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just shot a turkey. I'm basically a serial killer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ted Bundy started somewhere too, you know.

jokes.text: I stupidly agreed to go hunting with Stevie tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what David is doing.
jokes.punchline: The self-aware 'stupidly' is doing a lot of work — David knows it's wrong for him but went along with it anyway, and his parents' 'Hunting?!' reaction validates the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: A clean, efficient setup for the hunting plot. 'Stupidly' is the key word — David's self-aware admission that this is not going to go well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I feel terrible about shooting that deer.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, it happens. You shoot a deer, it dies. That's the whole point of shooting a deer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know, but I didn't mean to. It was an accident.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the deer didn't know that. As far as the deer's concerned, it's just as dead.

jokes.text: You and your nightgown can tippy-toe back to bed. I'm late.
jokes.setup: Johnny has been lurking in the dark room worried about David going hunting, wearing his nightgown.
jokes.punchline: David's dismissal of his father — specifically calling out the nightgown and using 'tippy-toe' — deflates Johnny's paternal concern with a single withering image.
jokes.explanation: 'You and your nightgown can tippy-toe back to bed' is a well-crafted put-down. 'Tippy-toe' is an unexpectedly childlike word choice that makes Johnny sound smaller, and combining it with the nightgown creates a complete, slightly absurd image. Strong economy and character integration — David is simultaneously brusque and precise.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, I have to say, you've really carved out an identity for yourself here in town.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Moira!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's quite impressive, really. Before you moved here, I wouldn't have recognized you in a lineup.

jokes.text: Elton John used to have an annual hunt at his place in Windsor, but that was more about the lunch.
jokes.setup: Ronnie asks David if he's ever killed before.
jokes.punchline: David references Elton John's hunt as his only hunting experience, then immediately clarifies it 'was more about the lunch' — suggesting he attended a celebrity hunt and ignored the hunting entirely.
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The Elton John name-drop works here because it's not just name-dropping — it tells a whole story. David was at Elton's hunt, focused on the lunch, and is now treating this as relevant hunting experience. 'That was more about the lunch' is a perfect understatement. Very high character integration — this is a joke only David Rose could deliver. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in quality.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I suppose I have been known to reinvent myself. Several times. In several countries. But that's not a flaw, that's called being versatile.

jokes.text: (Yelling) Turkey virgin!
jokes.setup: David has revealed he's never killed before.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie immediately announces this to everyone present with enthusiastic volume — 'Turkey virgin!' — a hunting community equivalent of hazing the newcomer.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — two words that get a big laugh. 'Turkey virgin' is funny both as a concept and in the specificity of 'turkey' (not just 'virgin hunter'). Ronnie's enthusiasm in shouting it amplifies the cringe/delight of David's situation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: If I have to shop at a big box store, I will simply cease to exist.

jokes.text: Does this come in a slimmer cut?
jokes.setup: David is being outfitted in hunting coveralls in the field.
jokes.punchline: His first concern about hunting gear is the silhouette. In camouflage coveralls in a field, David asks if they come slimmer.
jokes.explanation: A perfect six-word joke. David's fashion anxiety in the literal wilderness is maximally character-specific. 'Slimmer cut' while dressed for deer hunting is the exact right word choice — he's not asking about comfort or functionality, but fit. One of the episode's most efficiently constructed character jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, I need you to promise me something. If I ever become a burden, I want you to shoot me.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh Moira, I could never do that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Please, Jocelyn. I'm serious.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I wouldn't even know where to get a gun. Roland has all the guns.

jokes.text: Bob has a little prayer here he'd like to offer up. Just a little convo with the big man upstairs for a second.
jokes.setup: The hunting group is about to begin.
jokes.punchline: Roland's casual framing of a pre-hunt prayer as 'a little convo with the big man upstairs' — reducing solemn religious ritual to friendly small talk — is breezy and character-specific.
jokes.explanation: Roland's folksy reduction of prayer to a 'convo' is light character comedy. It establishes Roland and Bob's small-town religiosity while being gently comic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why is hunting appealing to you?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, it's just something to do. Gets you out of the house.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There are other ways to get out of the house that don't involve killing things.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, literally anything else. Although, I suppose it's no stranger than the bizarre tradition of drunk Carole King performances that seem to happen every time we go to the cottage.

jokes.text: David awkwardly joins the prayer circle mid-prayer, lid of something snapping loudly in the solemn moment.
jokes.setup: Bob is in the middle of a sincere prayer over the hunt.
jokes.punchline: David, caught off-guard, joins in — and a lid snaps loudly during the solemn moment, breaking the reverence.
jokes.explanation: A visual/physical cringe beat — David's out-of-place awkwardness in a sincere community ritual. The loud snap during prayer is classic cringe timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You look like a Victorian ghost that died of disappointment.

jokes.text: David is handed a communion-style flask/alcohol during the prayer and visibly taken aback
jokes.setup: Bob is delivering a solemn prayer before the hunt; something is being passed around
jokes.punchline: The lid snapping open (audio cue) and David's apparent surprise suggests something unexpected is being offered/passed during the prayer — likely alcohol or something else incongruous
jokes.explanation: A visual/audio beat that's hard to fully read from transcript alone. The lid snap and David's reaction suggest something funny is happening, but it's inferred. Lower confidence on this one — scoring conservatively.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm traumatized from hunting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: At least when you kill a turkey, it's over quickly. With Moira's appearance, the suffering is prolonged.

jokes.text: So is it, like, a small town thing to end the first date with a kiss on the cheek?
jokes.setup: Alexis is debriefing her date with a friend (implied Mutt/Twyla context).
jokes.punchline: Alexis is so accustomed to escalating first-date behavior that a kiss on the cheek reads to her as a cultural anomaly specific to small towns, not just a restrained gentleman.
jokes.explanation: Alexis attributing Ted's restraint to small-town customs rather than normal behavior is a good character beat — she's genuinely confused that someone might not try to sleep with her immediately.

jokes.text: I mean, he might as well have shaken your hand. — Well, he did that too.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests a cheek kiss is as formal as a handshake.
jokes.punchline: Alexis confirms he also shook her hand — so Ted administered both a handshake AND a cheek kiss at the end of the date, completing the business-meeting-level formality.
jokes.explanation: Clean, efficient setup/punchline. The joke works because it confirms the hyperbolic comparison — yes, he did actually shake her hand. 'He did that too' is perfectly deadpan. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: Yeah! I actually introduced him to Beyonce on the car ride on the way home, so that was very special.
jokes.setup: Someone asks if anything 'got crazy' on the date.
jokes.punchline: Alexis considers playing Beyoncé for Ted in the car 'very special' and somewhat equivalent to 'getting crazy' — her standards for excitement have become completely untethered.
jokes.explanation: 'I introduced him to Beyoncé' meaning she played him Beyoncé music in the car is a layered joke — the phrase implies a personal introduction (Alexis's world) but the reality is mundane. 'That was very special' as a solemn assessment of playing someone a Beyoncé song is perfect Alexis character comedy.

jokes.text: Mutt arrives with a smoothie for Alexis and gives a forced/awkward kiss — clearly more than just a friend.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just been discussing her date with Ted — the handshake and cheek kiss.
jokes.punchline: Mutt shows up and gives what the script calls a 'forced kiss' — a physical contrast to Ted's restrained behavior, while also being awkward given the conversation happening.
jokes.explanation: A visual/physical beat that contrasts with the Ted handshake discussion. Works as ironic juxtaposition — Alexis just said 'it's not a thing' with Mutt, and he arrives with physical affection. Functional comedy, not exceptional.

jokes.text: So how are your bowels? Good and tight?
jokes.setup: David arrives at the hunting spot and Roland greets him.
jokes.punchline: Roland's opener is a bowel inquiry — this is how he checks if David is ready for the hunt. The specificity of 'good and tight' is both crass and somehow practical.
jokes.explanation: Roland's complete lack of social filter around bodily functions is a consistent character trait. 'Good and tight?' as a greeting/wellness check is very Roland. The economy is good — it's short and specific.

jokes.text: You kinda seem like an indoors-y sort of guy to me. You know, delicate. Fragile. Scrimpy.
jokes.setup: Roland checks if David has the stomach for hunting.
jokes.punchline: Roland's list of descriptors for David — 'delicate, fragile, scrimpy' — culminates in 'scrimpy,' a word that may not be standard but perfectly suggests something both skinny and diminished.
jokes.explanation: 'Scrimpy' is the comedy here — it feels like Roland invented or misused a word, which makes it funnier than a more common insult. The escalating trio 'delicate, fragile, scrimpy' builds nicely.

jokes.text: Thank you. (David responds to being called delicate, fragile, and scrimpy)
jokes.setup: Roland has just called David 'delicate, fragile, scrimpy.'
jokes.punchline: David responds with a completely sincere-sounding 'Thank you' — either mishearing the insults as compliments or deadpanning through them.
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum economy. The joke is David's complete refusal to engage with the insult on Roland's terms — by saying 'Thank you,' he either genuinely takes the description as flattery (he might consider 'delicate' a compliment) or deflects with utter calm. Perfect character beat.

jokes.text: Uh, yeah, but it wasn't this... heavy, which makes me think that Elton's was actually a toy.
jokes.setup: Ronnie asks if David has ever held a gun before.
jokes.punchline: David confirms he has — at Elton's hunt — but the gun being lighter leads him to the conclusion that Elton John's gun was a toy, casually impugning the sincerity of Elton's famous annual hunt.
jokes.explanation: A superb callback and escalation of the Elton John bit. The logic is absurdist but perfectly David: the gun was lighter, therefore it must have been a toy. This also retroactively reframes the earlier Elton John story — not just 'more about lunch,' but possibly involving a toy gun. One of the episode's most rewatchable moments.

jokes.text: Treat it like it's a newborn baby. — Have to admit, not into babies so it's kind of a guessing game for me.
jokes.setup: Someone instructs David to hold the gun gently, like a newborn baby.
jokes.punchline: David's counter that he has no reference point for how to hold a baby — so this instruction is useless to him.
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with good character integration. David's frank admission that babies are outside his experience makes a practical instruction useless. 'Kind of a guessing game for me' is a nice understated button.

jokes.text: Oh, you got soft hands. — Okay, thank you. Thanks.
jokes.setup: The hunting instructor handles David's hands while positioning the gun.
jokes.punchline: David's almost grateful, slightly flustered 'Okay, thank you. Thanks.' in response to a gun-safety assessment of his hands is funny — he takes 'soft hands' as a compliment.
jokes.explanation: Another version of David's habit of accepting assessments as compliments (cf. 'Thank you' to 'delicate/fragile/scrimpy'). Not as strong as that beat but consistent character work.

jokes.text: Oh, you got soft hands. / Okay, thank you. Thanks.
jokes.setup: Roland is helping David grip the gun properly
jokes.punchline: Roland's observation about David's soft hands is clearly meant as a comment on his lack of outdoor labor; David accepts it as a compliment with two layers of thanks
jokes.explanation: David's double 'thank you' for the soft hands observation is a small but perfectly Davidian moment — he takes pride in an attribute that implies he's never done hard physical work. The redundancy ('Okay, thank you. Thanks.') suggests genuine appreciation.

jokes.text: Do what? Have fun? Smell like manure? Get called 'scrimpy'? Who wouldn't wanna sign up for this?
jokes.setup: Stevie tells David he doesn't have to do this.
jokes.punchline: David lists three things he's experiencing — fun (sarcastic), manure smell, being called scrimpy — and wraps it in 'who wouldn't wanna sign up for this?' The rhetorical question is the sarcastic bow.
jokes.explanation: The list structure works well — fun (ironic), manure (real), scrimpy (callback). David's sarcasm is at peak form. The escalation from abstract to specific makes this better than a generic complaint.

jokes.text: I wanna say Tanya Harding. — Do you? How about... — Jocelyn! — Oh my God, that's right! Why Jocelyn?
jokes.setup: Moira appears wearing the salon wig. Johnny tries to identify who she looks like.
jokes.punchline: He says Tanya Harding, then corrects himself to Jocelyn — Moira having been given Jocelyn's signature look at Jocelyn's own recommended salon.
jokes.explanation: The Tanya Harding red herring before landing on Jocelyn is a perfect comedic beat — Tanya Harding is already a funny choice, but Jocelyn is funnier because it's specific to the episode's stakes. The reveal that the salon gives everyone Jocelyn's look is a great structural payoff. Moira's horrified 'Why Jocelyn?' completes it.

jokes.text: Because everyone who comes out of that salon looks just like Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Moira asks why she looks like Jocelyn specifically.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's revelation that this is just how the salon operates — it has one style, and that style is Jocelyn.
jokes.explanation: The idea that a small-town salon produces a single, identical look — Jocelyn — is a lovely piece of small-town satire. 'Everyone looks just like Jocelyn' is the episode's central visual gag fully articulated.

jokes.text: No, it's a dead possum. Yes, it's a wig, John.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if it's Moira's wig.
jokes.punchline: Moira's sarcastic 'No, it's a dead possum' before confirming it's a wig — her contempt for the question (and the wig) expressed through absurdist sarcasm.
jokes.explanation: 'No, it's a dead possum' is a genuinely good Moira line — the wild absurdity of the alternative (dead possum on her head) paired with her complete disdain for Johnny's question. Economy is excellent. High quotability.

jokes.text: She's coming over. I just told a joke. / You did? (Laughs) You did!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is approaching; Moira needs to perform happiness and tells Johnny she 'just told a joke'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's genuine surprise — 'You did? You did!' — as if Moira telling a joke is a notable and unexpected event is both funny in itself and revealing of their dynamic
jokes.explanation: The meta beat of Moira announcing she just told a joke (so Johnny will laugh on cue) and Johnny's genuine surprise is a good character moment. The implication is that Moira making jokes is a rare enough occurrence to warrant acknowledgment.

jokes.text: I'm doing this for Jocelyn because she paid for it, and it was her 'little treat,' and I didn't have the heart to rip it off my head much less torch it. So, now, until I get in the shower, I'm stuck looking like every other inmate in this godforsaken prison.
jokes.setup: Moira explains why she's still wearing the offensive wig in public.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'I didn't rip it off' to 'torch it' to 'inmates in this godforsaken prison' — Moira's description of Schitt's Creek as a prison is the darkly funny peak.
jokes.explanation: Moira's dramatic escalation — kindness to Jocelyn framed as martyrdom — culminating in 'every other inmate in this godforsaken prison' is strong character comedy. The word 'inmate' for town resident is dark and very Moira.

jokes.text: She's coming over. I just told a joke. — You did? (laughs) You did!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is approaching and Moira needs to look natural.
jokes.punchline: Johnny claims he just told a joke (presumably to explain Moira's forced laughter) — Moira's surprised 'You did?' followed by genuine laughing confirmation is funny because it implies Johnny rarely tells jokes.
jokes.explanation: A funny little meta beat — the characters performing laughter for social camouflage, with Moira's surprise that Johnny told a joke being an unintentional insult. Works well within the scene.

jokes.text: Well, which beautiful wife are we talking about here, huh?
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks what Johnny thinks of his 'beautiful wife' (with the terrible wig).
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'Which beautiful wife?' implies he has multiple — but really sidesteps the direct question about the wig while being gallant. It's a perfect diplomatic non-answer.
jokes.explanation: A deft response that's both gallant (multiple beautiful wives = she's always beautiful) and evasive (doesn't have to say the wig looks good). Good economy and structure.

jokes.text: Of course, as an actress, I can imagine what kind of sad character might... But not necessarily.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to be gracious about her wig while Jocelyn is present, saying she can imagine it being freeing.
jokes.punchline: Moira starts to describe what 'sad character' might wear this wig — clearly describing Jocelyn implicitly — then poorly self-corrects with 'But not necessarily.'
jokes.explanation: Moira's inability to hide her contempt even when trying is perfect cringe comedy. She accidentally implies the look belongs to a 'sad character,' realizes she's describing Jocelyn, and adds 'But not necessarily' — which is far too late and too unconvincing. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: We have to get drinks. Several drinks, don't you think? Let's celebrate! Let's... celebrate drinking!
jokes.setup: The conversation is spiraling as Moira's 'sad character' comment threatens to offend Jocelyn.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's increasingly desperate social rescue culminates in 'Let's celebrate drinking!' — a non-reason to celebrate that reveals pure panic.
jokes.explanation: 'Let's celebrate drinking!' is a funny panic move — Johnny's social rescue attempt overshoots 'let's get drinks' into 'drinking itself is the celebration,' revealing desperation.

jokes.text: It's looking at me. — That's it. You're locked in. It's just you and him. — (Turkey gobbles) — Okay... (sharp inhale) — Just point and shoot, David. — Hard to do when you're talking, Bob. — There's no one talking now. — Bob, you're talking. — What? — Stop talking! — Too much talking! — Shoot the bird, David! Shoot it!
jokes.setup: David has finally lined up a shot at a turkey.
jokes.punchline: Everyone starts talking at once telling him not to listen to people talking — the ironic escalation of noise around someone trying to concentrate culminates in chaos.
jokes.explanation: The escalating contradiction — telling someone to stop listening to talking by talking more — is a well-constructed comedic spiral. 'Hard to do when you're talking, Bob' is a sharp line in the chaos. The whole sequence builds to a satisfying blowout before the shot. Good structure.

jokes.text: I feel like one of the Manson girls.
jokes.setup: David has just shot the turkey — apparently in the neck, not cleanly.
jokes.punchline: David's response to having killed something is to compare himself to Charles Manson's followers — invoking cult murder to describe his emotional state after an accidental turkey-neck shot.
jokes.explanation: The best line in the episode. 'I feel like one of the Manson girls' is everything a great David Rose line should be: unexpected, dark, specific, and perfectly calibrated. It's not 'I feel like a murderer' — it's specifically the Manson girls, which implies a particular blend of horror, complicity, and being swept up in something beyond your control. The economy is perfect. Highly quotable and endlessly rewatchable. Comparable in quality to the 9-10 anchor examples.

jokes.text: Even tougher when you shoot it in the neck 'cause then it takes longer to die.
jokes.setup: Ronnie says 'first time's tough' in response to David's horror.
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's follow-up clarification — that David's situation is SPECIFICALLY worse because a neck shot means a slower death — is delivered with complete matter-of-fact calm.
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's absolutely unbothered delivery of information that deepens David's horror is excellent character comedy. 'Then it takes longer to die' is stated as a practical addendum, not as cruel taunting, which makes it funnier. Good character integration for Ronnie.

jokes.text: So, now what? Do we just... wait until its Turkey family comes and shoot them too?
jokes.setup: Ronnie says to 'just let it bleed out.'
jokes.punchline: David's horrified extrapolation of the hunting logic — that they'll now ambush the grieving turkey family — treats turkeys as having social/family structures worthy of moral consideration.
jokes.explanation: David's moral horror at hunting expressed as 'the Turkey family comes' is endearing and funny — the domestication of wildlife into family units mirrors how David anthropomorphized the cricket. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Or one of your fashion-y blouses shrinks in the dryer, and you'll have to go to Janine's or get a shirt at the Blouse Barn, just like the rest of us.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is giving Moira sincere advice about accepting small-town life.
jokes.punchline: The 'Blouse Barn' — a store name that's inherently comic — lands as the small-town fashion alternative to Moira's luxury wardrobe.
jokes.explanation: 'Blouse Barn' is a funny name for a store — the agricultural metaphor applied to women's clothing — and its introduction as the alternative to Moira's life is good fish-out-of-water comedy.

jokes.text: But if you ever... Ever catch me shopping at the Blouse Barn, you must shoot me in the temple. Promise? — Promise. — My son is hunting right now with your husband, so I... I know you people have guns.
jokes.setup: Moira concedes Jocelyn's point about adapting to small-town life but draws a hard line at the Blouse Barn.
jokes.punchline: Moira requests to be shot in the temple if she ever shops at the Blouse Barn — then grounds this in the very specific, practical knowledge that Jocelyn's people have guns (because David is currently hunting with Roland).
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The suicide-by-Blouse-Barn request is very Moira — deeply dramatic and self-aware. But the real genius is the second half: Moira casually references the hunting plot to establish that Jocelyn's family literally has guns, making the request practical rather than theoretical. The cross-storyline callback ('My son is hunting right now') elevates this from a character line to sharp structural comedy. One of the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: What about what we just did makes you think 'yeah, you know what? I wanna do this year after year after year'? Was it the lying in the mud part or was it wearing the clothes that smell like menthol cigarettes and body odor?
jokes.setup: The hunting trip is over. Stevie asks (implicitly) about the experience.
jokes.punchline: David's catalogue of grievances — mud, and clothes smelling of 'menthol cigarettes and body odor' — delivered as a genuine inquiry into what possible appeal the annual event holds.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'menthol cigarettes and body odor' as the scent profile of the hunting coveralls is excellent. David's framing of 'year after year after year' as an incomprehensible tradition is good character work.

jokes.text: 'Cause usually it's more fun. Usually Ray comes and gets wasted and spends the whole day singing Carole King ballads.
jokes.setup: David questions what makes the annual turkey shoot worthwhile.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's defense of the turkey shoot is that normally Ray gets drunk and sings Carole King — a wonderfully specific and absurd picture of what 'fun' looks like at a small-town turkey hunt.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of Carole King (not just 'singing' but specifically Carole King ballads) is perfect — it's the exact right artist for a wasted small-town man at a turkey hunt. The image is complete, strange, and funny. Good originality for a throwaway line.

jokes.text: David, I... Need a towel, please. — Is that a new wig? Because... You are reminding me of someone. — Towel, please.
jokes.setup: Moira enters through the adjoining door, still wearing the wig.
jokes.punchline: David doesn't immediately recognize his own mother due to the wig — he asks if it's a new wig because she's reminding him of someone (Jocelyn). The towel callback from the opening is reprised.
jokes.explanation: The two plotlines converge — David encountering the Jocelyn-wig Moira while she continues the towel running gag. The 'reminding me of someone' without naming Jocelyn is a nice delayed punchline.

jokes.text: I need my towels. Uh, what... who did this to you?!
jokes.setup: David processes Moira's wig.
jokes.punchline: 'Who did this to you?!' — framing a salon visit as an act of violence inflicted upon Moira, which from Moira's perspective is accurate.
jokes.explanation: The crime-victim framing ('Who did this to you?!') is funny because it validates Moira's perception of the salon experience. Short and well-delivered.

jokes.text: You went to Janine's. — Yes. Jocelyn took me to... — (gasps) Jocelyn! You look like Jocelyn!
jokes.setup: David has been suspicious of the wig. He hears it was Jocelyn's suggestion.
jokes.punchline: David immediately connects the dots and announces 'You look like Jocelyn!' — his gasp of recognition mirroring the audience's.
jokes.explanation: David landing on the same conclusion as Johnny — 'You look like Jocelyn!' — is a satisfying payoff. His gasp of horror-recognition is well-deployed. Strengthened by being a callback to Johnny's identical realization.

jokes.text: Jocelyn. And what's your excuse?
jokes.setup: David has just identified Moira's wig as 'Jocelyn-looking' and expressed horror.
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots immediately to David's appearance — 'And what's your excuse?' — implying he also looks terrible, deflecting her own embarrassment.
jokes.explanation: Four words that turn the tables perfectly. Moira's counterattack using David's hunting-disheveled appearance is sharp and quick. The pivot from 'you look like Jocelyn' to 'what's your excuse' is well-structured.

jokes.text: Oh, um... I shot a bird in the neck today. It was an indelible image that I had scarred in my mind until I saw what was happening up there with you.
jokes.setup: Moira asks what David's excuse is for looking terrible.
jokes.punchline: David explains his trauma (shooting the turkey in the neck) but pivots to saying Moira's wig was actually MORE disturbing — the trauma of seeing her wig has displaced his hunting horror.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural joke that ties both plots together. The idea that Moira's wig is more traumatic than shooting a turkey in the neck — expressed as genuine relief ('until I saw what was happening up there') — is a perfect episode button. Strong character integration and a satisfying convergence of the two storylines.

tmdb_overview: Following an incident where David is unable to kill an insect in his room, Stevie, as a joke, invites David along on Roland's annual turkey shoot.
insight: Character-driven cringe peaks early: 52 jokes sustain momentum despite middling craft scores.
